title: The right way to disagree with a friend
summary: Argue for a specific, principled approach to disagreeing with someone you care about, using a real or observed example.
topic: The right way to disagree with a friend
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing for one clear principle that should guide disagreements between friends. Describe a moment — your own or one you observed — when disagreement was handled either well or badly. Use modal verbs, at least one concession with 'although' or 'however', and a second-conditional sentence.
hintStarters: What I've noticed is that most friendships don't end because of the disagreement itself, but…
I'd argue that the first thing you should do when you disagree is…
If people separated the person from the argument, they would…

hintVocab.meaningVi: gây sự, tìm cớ cãi nhau

hintVocab.phrase: to separate the person from the argument
hintVocab.meaningVi: tách con người ra khỏi tranh luận
lắng nghe ai đó đến cùng

hintVocab.phrase: to put words in someone's mouth

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự bất hòa, cãi vã dẫn đến xa cách
chấp nhận sự khác biệt quan điểm
tôn trọng góc nhìn của ai đó

sampleText:
Two colleagues I know — both friends since university in Kraków — stopped speaking for nearly a year over a disagreement about money. Neither of them had lost their temper. They had simply talked over each other, each convinced they were being clear, until one sent a message that the other read as an accusation. What broke them apart was not the original argument but the fact that neither had bothered to hear the other out. I'd argue that this is the most common way friendships get damaged: not by a single explosion, but by a slow failure to listen.

Although many people believe that keeping quiet is a way of protecting a friendship, I think the opposite is closer to the truth. Unexpressed disagreements do not disappear — they settle at the bottom and change the temperature of every conversation that follows. The right approach, in my view, is to make your point calmly and specifically, without putting words in the other person's mouth. If people separated the person from the argument, they would find that most disputes are about a misunderstanding, not about a genuine difference in values. The moment you say 'you always do this' or 'you never listen', you have stopped arguing about the problem and started attacking the person, and that is very hard to recover from.

However, knowing the right thing to do is much easier than doing it. Friendship carries history, and history makes everything more charged. What I have found, watching friends navigate disagreements in places as different as Buenos Aires and Helsinki, is that the ones who handle it best tend to wait — not for days, but for a few hours — before they respond to something that stings. They come back to the conversation when they can disagree without performing anger. They also accept that backing down on a point is not the same as losing. A friendship where both people can stand their ground and still clear the air is stronger than one where every argument ends in false agreement. The goal is not to win; it is to still be friends when it is over.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: lắng nghe người kia đến cùng
sự thất bại âm thầm trong việc lắng nghe

sampleAnnotations.phrase: putting words in the other person's mouth
separated the person from the argument
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tách con người ra khỏi tranh luận
lùi bước về một quan điểm

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for a general principle
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If people separated the person from the argument, they would find that most disputes are about a misunderstanding…' dùng quá khứ đơn + would để miêu tả một kết quả giả định — điều có thể xảy ra nếu mọi người thay đổi hành vi của họ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If people separated the person from the argument, they would find that most disputes are about a misunderstanding…' uses past simple + would to describe a hypothetical outcome — something that could happen if people changed their behaviour.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for observed experience
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What I have found, watching friends navigate disagreements…' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn đạt một quan sát tích lũy từ nhiều tình huống khác nhau, không gắn với một sự kiện cụ thể.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What I have found, watching friends navigate disagreements…' uses the present perfect to express an observation built up from many different situations, not tied to one specific event.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hai đồng nghiệp tôi quen — cả hai là bạn bè từ thời đại học ở Kraków — đã ngừng nói chuyện gần một năm vì một tranh cãi về tiền bạc. Không ai trong số họ mất bình tĩnh. Họ chỉ đơn giản là nói chồng lên nhau, mỗi người đều chắc chắn rằng mình đang nói rõ ràng, cho đến khi một người gửi một tin nhắn mà người kia đọc như một lời buộc tội. Điều khiến họ xa nhau không phải là tranh cãi ban đầu mà là thực tế là không ai trong số họ chịu lắng nghe người kia đến cùng. Tôi cho rằng đây là cách phổ biến nhất khiến tình bạn bị tổn hại: không phải bởi một vụ bùng nổ duy nhất, mà bởi sự thất bại âm thầm trong việc lắng nghe.

Mặc dù nhiều người tin rằng giữ im lặng là cách bảo vệ tình bạn, tôi nghĩ điều ngược lại mới gần với sự thật hơn. Những bất đồng chưa được nói ra không biến mất — chúng lắng xuống và thay đổi nhiệt độ của mọi cuộc trò chuyện sau đó. Cách tiếp cận đúng, theo quan điểm của tôi, là trình bày quan điểm một cách bình tĩnh và cụ thể, mà không bịa lời cho người kia. Nếu mọi người tách con người ra khỏi tranh luận, họ sẽ thấy rằng hầu hết các tranh chấp là về sự hiểu lầm, không phải về sự khác biệt giá trị thực sự. Khoảnh khắc bạn nói 'bạn luôn làm vậy' hoặc 'bạn không bao giờ lắng nghe', bạn đã ngừng tranh luận về vấn đề và bắt đầu tấn công vào con người, và điều đó rất khó phục hồi.

Tuy nhiên, biết điều đúng đắn cần làm dễ hơn nhiều so với thực tế làm nó. Tình bạn mang theo lịch sử, và lịch sử khiến mọi thứ trở nên mang nhiều cảm xúc hơn. Điều tôi đã nhận thấy, khi quan sát bạn bè vượt qua những bất đồng ở những nơi khác nhau như Buenos Aires và Helsinki, là những người xử lý tốt nhất thường đợi — không phải nhiều ngày, mà vài giờ — trước khi họ phản hồi điều gì đó làm họ tổn thương. Họ quay lại cuộc trò chuyện khi họ có thể bất đồng mà không thể hiện sự tức giận. Họ cũng chấp nhận rằng lùi bước về một quan điểm không giống với việc thua. Một tình bạn mà cả hai người có thể giữ vững lập trường và vẫn giải tỏa hiểu lầm thì mạnh hơn một tình bạn mà mỗi cuộc tranh luận kết thúc bằng sự đồng ý giả tạo. Mục tiêu không phải là chiến thắng; đó là vẫn còn là bạn bè khi mọi thứ kết thúc.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: If people separated the person from the argument, they would find most disputes are about misunderstandings.
If people separate the person from the argument, they would found most disputes.
If people will separate the person, they would find most disputes.
If people separated the person, they will find most disputes about misunderstandings.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + 'would + base verb' in the result clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Past simple after 'if', then 'would' + bare infinitive.

options: Although many people believe keeping quiet, but I think the opposite.
Although many people believe keeping quiet protects a friendship, I think the opposite is true.
Many people believe keeping quiet, although, I think the opposite.
Although, many people believe keeping quiet protects a friendship I think the opposite.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no extra 'but', and no comma directly after 'although'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' introduces a subordinate clause; the main clause follows without 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly?
options: What I have find is that the ones who handle it best tend to wait.
What I founded is that the ones who handles it best tend to wait.
What I have found is that the ones who handle it best tend to wait.
What I have been finding yesterday is that the ones tend to wait.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = have/has + past participle; 'found' is the past participle of 'find'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'however' to introduce a contrast?
options: However knowing the right thing to do is much easier than doing it.
Knowing the right thing to do however is much easier than doing it.
Knowing the right thing, however to do is much easier than doing it.
However, knowing the right thing to do is much easier than doing it.
mcQuestions.explanation: When 'however' begins a sentence as a discourse marker, it is followed by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: Sentence-initial 'however' needs a comma immediately after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best uses a modal to make a recommendation?
options: You should make your point calmly and specifically.
You should to make your point calmly and specifically.
You should making your point calmly and specifically.
You should made your point calmly and specifically.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal verbs like 'should' are followed by the bare infinitive, not 'to' or a participle.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Should' is directly followed by the base form of the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase is a correct collocation for ending a dispute?
options: clear the atmosphere
clear the air
clean the air
empty the air
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Clear the air' is the fixed idiom for resolving tension or misunderstanding.
mcQuestions.hint: This is a fixed idiom; only one verb collocates correctly with 'the air' in this sense.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly avoids a double subject in a relative clause?
options: A friendship where both people they can stand their ground and still clear the air.
A friendship where both people who can stand their ground and still clear the air.
A friendship where both people can stand their ground and still clear the air.
A friendship which both people can they stand their ground.
mcQuestions.explanation: In a relative clause, do not repeat the subject with a pronoun ('they') or add an extra relative pronoun.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'where', use the subject directly — no need to repeat 'they' or add 'who'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure correctly in a list?
options: They come back, disagreeing without anger, and accept that backing down is not losing.
They come back, disagree without anger, and accepting that backing down is not losing.
They comes back, disagree without anger, and accept that backing down is not losing.
They come back, disagree without anger, and accept that backing down is not losing.
mcQuestions.explanation: All three verbs in the list should be in the same base form: 'come', 'disagree', 'accept'.
mcQuestions.hint: Check that all verbs in a series share the same form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option expresses a negative result without using 'not'?
options: Neither of them had lost their temper.
Both of them had not lost their temper.
None of them had lose their temper.
Either of them had lost not their temper.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Neither' already contains the negative meaning; no extra 'not' is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Neither' is inherently negative — it replaces 'not either'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best introduces a personal observation in a B1 essay?
options: I am arguing that this is the most common way friendships get damaged.
I'd argue that this is the most common way friendships get damaged.
I argued that this was the most common way friendships were getting damaged.
I would be arguing that this is the most common way friendships get damaged.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'I'd argue that…' (contracted 'would') is a natural, softened opinion phrase at B1.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'I'd argue that' for a polite, confident opinion — not the continuous form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses gerund and infinitive correctly after 'stop'?
options: You have stopped to argue about the problem and started to attack the person.
You have stopped to arguing about the problem and started attacking the person.
You have stopped arguing about the problem and started attacking the person.
You have stopped argue about the problem and started to attacking the person.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stop + gerund' = stop an activity in progress; 'start + gerund' = begin an activity.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Stop doing' means to cease an action; both 'stop' and 'start' can take gerunds.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Are there types of disagreement — political, moral, financial — where friendship cannot survive the difference of opinion? Where would you draw that line?
